perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the term "associates" or "contacts" shall be defined as: in the case of a business entity its officers, directors, affiliates, subsidiaries, associated entities, and any other business entity in which the business entity owns five percent (5%) or more of the outstanding equity interest.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the term "associates" or "contacts" shall be defined as: in the case of a business entity its officers, directors, and employees, regardless of ownership or affiliation.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "associates" is broadened to include all employees, conflicting with the standard legal interpretation of "affiliates" and potentially violating Pennsylvania's business association laws by blurring the lines between employees and business entities.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law defines business associations and their structures. This modification, by including all employees as 'associates' irrespective of their association with the entity, can create confusion about the legal status and liabilities of the employees within the agreement, potentially contradicting the statute's definitions of corporate structure and agency.

Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 15 - CORPORATIONS AND UNINCORPORATED ASSOCIATIONS Chapter 11 - General Provisions Section 1103 - Definitions Universal Citation: 15 PA Cons Stat § 1103 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 1103. Definitions. (a) General definitions.-- Subject to additional definitions contained in subsequent provisions of this subpart that are applicable to specific provisions of this subpart, the following words and phrases when used in Part I (relating to preliminary provisions) or in this subpart shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Act" or "action." (Deleted by amendment). "Amendment." An amendment of the articles. "Articles." The original articles of incorporation, all amendments thereof and any other articles, statements or certificates permitted or required to be filed in the Department of State by sections 108 (relating to change in location or status of registered office provided by agent) and 138 (relating to statement of correction), Chapter 3 (relating to entity transactions) or this subpart and including what have heretofore been designated by law as certificates of incorporation or charters. If an amendment of the articles or a statement filed under Chapter 3 restates articles in their entirety, thenceforth the "articles" shall not include any prior documents and any certificate issued by the department with respect thereto shall so state. "Authorized shares." The shares of all classes that the corporation is authorized to
perturbation.justification: The original contract language defines "associates" or "contacts" in the context of a business entity, limiting it to officers, directors, affiliates, subsidiaries, associated entities, and those with a significant equity interest. The changed text broadens this definition to include all employees, regardless of ownership or affiliation. The Law Explanation states that this could create confusion about the legal status and liabilities of employees, potentially contradicting Pennsylvania statutes' definitions of corporate structure and agency. Scraped Law Snippet 2, from 15 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 1103, defines "articles" and other terms related to corporations and unincorporated associations, suggesting that Pennsylvania law has specific definitions for business entities and their components. By including all employees as 'associates', the modified contract deviates from standard legal interpretations and introduces potential conflict with the established corporate structure definitions, which is further enforced by the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The Agreement is valid and effective for all purposes, business, communications, negotiations, disclosures and transactions of whatever nature between the Parties for a period of two (2) years from the effective date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement is valid and effective for all purposes, business, communications, negotiations, disclosures and transactions of whatever nature between the Parties indefinitely from the effective date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the agreement to last "indefinitely" directly contradicts the Pennsylvania statute of limitations, which imposes time limits on contract claims, making the enforceability uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law, 42 Pa.C.S. § 5525, sets a six-year statute of limitations for contract claims. An 'indefinite' agreement duration contradicts this law, potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable after the statutory period for bringing claims has expired.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5525 (2022) - Four year limitation :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 42 - JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE Chapter 55 - Limitation of Time Section 5525 - Four year limitation Universal Citation: 42 PA Cons Stat § 5525 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5525. Four year limitation. (a) General rule.-- Except as provided for in subsection (b), the following actions and proceedings must be commenced within four years: (1) An action upon a contract, under seal or otherwise, for the sale, construction or furnishing of tangible personal property or fixtures. (2) Any action subject to 13 Pa.C.S. § 2725 (relating to statute of limitations in contracts for sale). (3) An action upon an express contract not founded upon an instrument in writing. (4) An action upon a contract implied in law, except an action subject to another limitation specified in this subchapter. (5) An action upon a judgment or decree of any court of the United States or of any state. (6) An action upon any official bond of a public official, officer or employee. (7) An action upon a negotiable or nonnegotiable bond, note or other similar instrument in writing. Where such an instrument is payable upon demand, the time within which an action on it must be commenced shall be computed from the later of either demand or any payment of principal of or interest on the instrument. (8) An action upon a contract, obligation or liability founded upon a writing not specified in paragraph (7), under seal or otherwise, except an action subject to another limitation specified in this subchapter.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original agreement to last 'indefinitely,' directly contradicting the statute of limitations for contract claims under Pennsylvania law. Specifically, 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5525 sets a four-year limitation for actions upon a contract. By setting the agreement to 'indefinitely,' the changed text undermines the enforceability of the agreement after the statutory period has expired.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of circumvention by the "Parties" involved in this transaction, either directly or indirectly, it is agreed and guaranteed that a monetary penalty will be paid by the person or persons engaged in or circumvention. This payment will additionally include all reasonable legal expenses incurred by the aggrieved party.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of circumvention by the "Parties" involved in this transaction, either directly or indirectly, it is agreed that a penalty will be determined at the discretion of the offended party. This determination is final and not subject to review.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the "monetary penalty" guarantee and introducing subjective determination of penalties violates due process and contract law principles, making the enforcement of such provision legally questionable in Pennsylvania.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the guarantee of a monetary penalty and introduces a subjective determination by the offended party, which is final and not subject to review. This violates due process rights under Article I, Section 11 of the Pennsylvania Constitution, as it denies the circumventing party the right to a fair hearing or appeal regarding the penalty imposed.

Article I, Pennsylvania Constitution From Ballotpedia Jump to: navigation , search Pennsylvania Constitution Preamble Articles I • II • III • IV • V • VI • VII • VIII • IX • X • XI • Schedule 1 • Schedule 2 Contents 1 Preamble 2 Section 1 3 Section 2 4 Section 3 5 Section 4 6 Section 5 7 Section 6 8 Section 7 9 Section 8 10 Section 9 11 Section 10 12 Section 11 13 Section 12 14 Section 13 15 Section 14 16 Section 15 17 Section 16 18 Section 17 19 Section 18 20 Section 19 21 Section 20 22 Section 21 23 Section 22 24 Section 23 25 Section 24 26 Section 25 27 Section 26 28 Section 27 29 Section 28 30 Section 29 30.1 Amendments 31 External links 32 Navigation Article I of the Pennsylvania Constitution is entitled Declaration of Rights and consists of 29 sections and a preamble. Preamble Text of Preamble: That the general, great and essential principles of liberty and free government may be recognized and unalterably established, WE DECLARE THAT Section 1 Text of Section 1: Inherent Rights of Mankind All men are born equally free and independent, and have certain inherent and indefeasible rights, among which are those of enjoying and defending life and liberty, of acquiring, possessing and protecting property and reputation, and of pursuing their own happiness. Section 2 Text of Section 2: Political Powers All power is inherent in the people, and all free governments are founded on their authority and instituted for their peace, safety and happiness. For the advancement of these ends they have at all times an inalienable and indefeasible right to alter, reform or abolish their government in such manner as they may think proper. Section 3 Text of Section 3: Religious Freedom All men have a natural and indefeasible right to worship Almighty God according to the dictates of their own consciences; no man can of right be compelled to attend, erect or support any place of worship or to maintain any ministry against his consent; no human authority can, in any case whatever, control or interfere with the rights of conscience, and no preference shall ever be given by law to any religious establishments or modes of worship. Section 4 Text of Section 4: Religion No person who acknowledges the being of a God and a future state of rewards and punishments shall, on account of his religious sentiments, be disqualifie
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a guaranteed monetary penalty with a penalty determined subjectively by the offended party, a determination that is final and not subject to review. This directly contradicts the principles of due process as it removes the right to a fair hearing or appeal, potentially violating Article I, Section 11 of the Pennsylvania Constitution, which ensures rights are protected. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions Article I of the Pennsylvania Constitution as a 'Declaration of Rights'. The removal of guaranteed monetary penalty and introducing a subjective, non-reviewable penalty imposition undermines fairness and due process. This creates a legally questionable scenario, making enforcement challenging due to the potential violation of due process rights.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by law and construed to be in accordance with the laws of the State of Pennsylvania applicable to contracts made and to be performed solely in such State by parties thereof. Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be adjudicated in arbitration under the rules of the American Arbitration Association. The prevailing party in any dispute shall be reimbursed reasonable attorneys fees.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed solely by the internal regulations of High Speed Net Solutions, without regard to any state or federal law. Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be resolved through a lottery system overseen by a designated representative of High Speed Net Solutions. The decision of the lottery is final and binding.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing Pennsylvania law with the company's internal regulations and replacing arbitration with a lottery system directly contradicts established contract law and dispute resolution norms, making the entire agreement unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes Pennsylvania law as the governing law and replaces it with company regulations and replaces arbitration with a lottery system, contradicting the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S. Code § 2), which recognizes the validity and enforceability of arbitration agreements. By replacing arbitration with a lottery, the modification undermines the legal framework for dispute resolution.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. perturbation.justification: The modified contract explicitly replaces the governing state law (Pennsylvania) with the company's internal regulations and replaces arbitration with a lottery system. This directly contradicts 9 U.S. Code § 2, which states that "a written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction...shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable." By replacing arbitration, which is legally recognized and enforceable, with a lottery system, the modification undermines the legal framework for dispute resolution. The original text agreed to arbitration; the changed text does not, establishing a contradiction.
